National Institute of Teaching  ·  Research & Evidence
Evidence Briefing  ·  April 2026

Who Gets a Good Pass? Mapping Disadvantaged Pupils' GCSE Outcomes in English Secondary Schools

Fewer than three in ten disadvantaged pupils achieve a grade 5+ in English and Maths. This briefing maps the gap — and the schools that are closing it.

Calum Davey  ·  April 2026

Full briefing with technical notes available as a PDF

↓ Download PDF

The Gap That Isn't Closing

Every year, fewer than three in ten disadvantaged pupils in England achieve a "good pass" — grade 5 or above in both GCSE English Language and Mathematics. Among their better-off classmates, it is more than five in ten.

That gap — around 27 percentage points — is wider today than it was before the pandemic. Despite billions of pounds in Pupil Premium funding, despite decades of school improvement effort, and despite a stated government commitment to halve it, the gap has barely moved.

This briefing uses the most recent published data to ask a more focused question: are there schools that are beating the odds? And if so, what can we learn from them?

The National Picture

What we mean by a "good pass": GCSE grade 5 or above in both English Language and Mathematics. This is the government's "strong pass" measure — one step above the grade 4 standard pass, and widely used as a threshold for A-level and higher education.

What we mean by "disadvantaged": Pupils eligible for free school meals at any point in the past six years, or who are looked after. This is the Pupil Premium definition — broader than current FSM eligibility, and better reflects persistent disadvantage.

26% Disadvantaged pupils
achieving a good pass
53% Non-disadvantaged pupils
achieving a good pass
27pp Gap between the two groups
(2023–24)

The Schools White Paper (February 2026) sets a national ambition to halve this gap — from around 27pp to approximately 13–14pp. On current trends, that would require disadvantaged pupils' pass rate to rise from 26% to around 40%, or a combination of improvement and narrowing from both ends.

The gap has widened since 2019, not narrowed. The 2018–19 figure was 25pp; the most recent published national figure (2023–24) is 27pp. The COVID years (2020–22) saw inflated grades across the board and are not comparable. But comparing 2018–19 directly with 2023–24, there has been no progress.

What 3,000 Schools Tell Us

The chart below shows every non-selective secondary school in England for which 2024–25 school-level data are available — around 3,100 schools in total. Each cell represents the density of schools in that area of the chart.

  • Horizontal axis: The percentage of a school's disadvantaged pupils who achieved a good pass.
  • Vertical axis: The gap at that school — how far disadvantaged pupils trail their non-disadvantaged classmates.

The vertical red dashed line marks 60% — the threshold used here to identify genuinely outstanding performance.

The mass of schools clusters in the 10–40% range for disadvantaged pass rates — consistent with the national average of 26%. Most also show a gap of 15–30pp.

There is a clear downward trend: schools with higher pass rates for disadvantaged pupils tend to have smaller gaps. But the relationship is modest — raising outcomes for disadvantaged pupils does not automatically compress the gap. Some high-performing schools still show a large gap because their non-disadvantaged pupils also do very well.

The 52 non-selective schools to the right of the red line — achieving 60%+ for disadvantaged pupils — are the focus of what follows.

Where Are the High-Performing Schools?

The map below shows those 52 schools plotted across England. Colour indicates religious character (faith schools in orange; non-faith in teal). Size indicates the number of disadvantaged pupils — larger circles mean larger cohorts.

The geographic concentration is striking. London dominates. Around two-thirds of the 52 schools are in the capital — particularly Inner and East London. Outside London, there are scattered examples in the North West and Yorkshire, but very few in the Midlands, South West, or East of England.

This is partly a structural story — London has more free schools, more academy chains with strong disadvantage track records, and a higher density of faith schools serving diverse urban communities. But it also raises a direct question: where is the equivalent provision in the rest of the country?

Who Are These Schools?

Several features characterise the 52 schools:

  • 25 are faith schools (48%) — compared with around 30% of state secondary schools nationally. Catholic, Church of England, and Islamic schools are all represented.
  • 33 are in London (63%) — particularly Inner and East London.
  • Many belong to strong academy chains: Star Academies, Ark Schools, Harris Federation, and Mossbourne Community Academy Trust are all represented. Eight of the 52 are in Star Academies alone.
  • Most are relatively large — with substantial disadvantaged cohorts. These are not small schools where a handful of pupils can move the headline number.

What they have in common

Strong chain membership. The majority belong to multi-academy trusts with an explicit, long-standing commitment to disadvantage. Star Academies, Ark, and Harris have documented approaches — structured curricula, high-dosage tutoring, extended school days, rigorous CPD. This is not accidental excellence; it is systematically built in.

The "London Effect." The concentration in London reflects two decades of sustained improvement effort in the capital. The London Challenge (2003–2011) transformed some of the country's worst-performing schools. Many of today's high-performing chains grew from that period. The effects persist — but they also show that context matters. London schools operate in a different labour market, funding environment, and policy ecosystem from much of the country.

Faith character. Catholic and Islamic schools appear disproportionately in this group. Research suggests this may relate to school ethos, parental engagement, and the motivational structures that faith schools can sustain. This finding is not straightforward to transfer — but it points to the value of strong, shared school culture.

Size and stability. These are mostly established schools with experienced leadership. The evidence suggests sustained, consistent performance over multiple years rather than a one-year spike.

Implications for the National Institute of Teaching

The Pupil Premium good pass gap is one of the most politically and professionally salient measures in English education. The government has committed to halving it. NIoT's work — on teacher development, school leadership, and evidence-based practice — sits directly in the frame.

  1. The gap is structural, not just instructional. The schools closing the gap combine strong curriculum design, coherent professional development, effective use of Pupil Premium funding, and sustained senior leadership commitment. NIoT's teacher development offer must be embedded in this broader ecology, not treated as a stand-alone intervention.
  2. The regional distribution is a challenge. Two-thirds of high-performing schools are in London. NIoT's national footprint — and its mission to reach teachers in areas of greatest need — points directly toward the areas where equivalent schools are absent: the East Midlands, South West, and coastal towns. A targeting question follows: should NIoT prioritise placements and partnerships in these underserved regions?
  3. The chain effect is real and transferable. Star Academies and Ark have demonstrated that sustained improvement for disadvantaged pupils is achievable at scale. Understanding how these chains develop their teachers — and how NIoT can learn from and work with them — is a live strategic question.
  4. The 60% threshold is not a ceiling. The national average is 26%. That 52 non-selective schools have cleared 60% — more than double the national rate — shows that the structural barriers are not insuperable. The Schools White Paper target of ~40% is achievable in principle, but will require more than incremental improvement across most of the country.

Technical Notes

Data: DfE Key Stage 4 Performance tables, 2024–25 (provisional) for school-level analysis. National headline figures (26%/53%/27pp gap) from DfE KS4 2023–24 — the most recent year with published national breakdowns by disadvantage group.

"Good pass": Grade 5+ in both English Language and Mathematics GCSE (engmath_95_percent in DfE tables). "Disadvantaged": Ever-FSM in past 6 years + looked-after children (pupil premium definition).

Non-selective schools: Identified via GIAS (Get Information About Schools) admissions policy data. n = 3,110 non-selective state-funded secondary schools with non-suppressed disadvantaged data. 2,275 schools suppressed (typically where the disadvantaged cohort is fewer than 6 pupils).

Map: England LA boundaries from martinjc/UK-GeoJSON. School coordinates from GIAS Easting/Northing (OSGB36), converted to WGS84. COVID caveat: 2019–20 to 2021–22 grades are not comparable with exam years and are excluded from trend analysis.

Analysis: Python (pandas, matplotlib, pyproj). Data collected April 2026.

Prepared April 2026  ·  Data: Department for Education, GIAS  ·  Analysis: Calum Davey
